briefing.opening: Good morning, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for June 26, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 European Heatwave Crisis: A Clear Warning of Climate Change
sections.content: Record-breaking, worst-ever heatwaves are occurring across Europe, paralyzing urban functions and leading to severe human and social damage, including dozens of deaths [3, 41, 141]. Scientists have analyzed that these heatwaves would have been 'virtually impossible' without human-induced climate change from fossil fuel emissions, and the average temperature was 3.5 degrees higher than similar heatwaves 50 years ago [7, 10, 12, 16, 21, 37, 116, 118, 119, 122, 123, 125, 126, 129, 130, 131, 132, 133, 134, 135, 136, 140, 142, 145]. As such, the Earth's 'heat stress' has fatally intensified over the past 50 years, making phenomena like daytime heatwaves and tropical nights commonplace [32]. This is a clear signal warning that the climate crisis is already profoundly impacting our society [137].

sections.title: ⚠️ Climate Change, Emerging as a Threat to Our Daily Lives and Industries
sections.content: Climate change poses direct threats to various aspects of our daily lives. In Jeju, a major broccoli producing region in Korea, abnormal high temperatures and frequent rainfall are making crops vulnerable to disease, leading to the development of new varieties [1, 9, 114]. The degradation of soybean seed quality and the need to strengthen responses to tick-borne infectious diseases have also been raised [20, 117, 121]. Furthermore, securing the safety of nuclear power plants is emerging as a critical task in the era of climate change [8], and new challenges are appearing, such as AI data centers becoming new targets for climate litigation worldwide [33]. Achieving carbon neutrality targets in the cement industry is becoming more difficult [50, 84], and research indicating that carbon absorption decreases as forests become fragmented demonstrates the complexity of carbon neutrality policies [25].

sections.title: 🌱 Diverse Domestic and International Efforts for Achieving Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Domestic and international efforts to address climate change are actively underway in various sectors. Many companies, including Daewoo E&C, Kwangdong Pharmaceutical, Tongyang Life Insurance, Kakao Games, SM, and Samsung Electronics, have published sustainability reports, declaring their commitment to climate change response and strengthening ESG management [2, 11, 15, 17, 24, 35, 36, 44, 99]. Pocheon City is promoting the establishment of a 'Climate Change and Carbon Neutrality Education Center' utilizing closed schools [6, 13, 45, 78]. Gwangmyeong City is expanding the ecosystem for carbon neutrality practices in daily life by providing free support for an electronic receipt system [47, 48, 49, 55, 56, 57, 60, 68, 77]. Additionally, the first 'Carbon Exchange' was launched on June 29, activating a market mechanism towards 2050 net-zero [19]. The establishment of the 'Korea Moss Carbon Neutrality Association' and the importance of 'transition finance' were also emphasized [53, 54].

sections.title: ⚡ Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Building a Stable Power Grid
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is a core pillar of climate change response, and its importance is becoming increasingly prominent. Companies continue their efforts to expand renewable energy, with Samsung Electronics achieving a 95% renewable energy transition rate and 67% of its water resource restoration target [99, 112]. Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEPCO) is building a power control system cooperation network to strengthen national power grid stability in the era of AI and renewable energy [96, 101, 103, 105, 106, 108]. The Korea Meteorological Administration (KMA) has launched solar radiation and wind speed forecasting services to assist in predicting renewable energy generation [92, 98, 109]. Korea Water Resources Corporation (K-water) is expanding various power generation projects, including accelerating 6.5 GW of floating solar power through a public-private cooperation system [102, 107]. However, securing grid flexibility and managing weather risks due to unprecedented electricity demand and the expansion of renewable energy remain crucial challenges [90].

briefing.closing: As confirmed in today's briefing, climate change is no longer a distant future problem but an ongoing threat, and at the same time, a driving force for creating new opportunities. To effectively respond to these threats and build a sustainable future, we must continue international cooperation and diverse domestic efforts. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: Jeju, a major broccoli producing region (73.8% of national cultivation area), is developing new disease-resistant varieties due to increased downy mildew outbreaks caused by recent abnormal high temperatures and frequent rainfall. The Jeju Agricultural Technology Institute plans to utilize molecular marker technology to develop genetic resources and establish a stable production base by 2028.

summary.phenomenon: In Jeju Island, a major broccoli producing region in South Korea (accounting for 73.8% of the national cultivation area), broccoli downy mildew is rampant due to recent abnormal high temperatures and frequent rainfall.
